coredata.dc:title: A dual-responsive sensor derived from carbonized polymer dots for the determination of cyanidin-3-O-glucoside
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Yu
Chen, Kaixin
Yang, Tingzhi
Wang, Yingshuang
You, Yueping
Li, Yang
Chen, Hulan
Tang, Zhentao
coredata.dc:description: Accurate detection of cyanidin-3-O-glucoside (C3G) is crucial for ensuring food safety and optimizing quality control measures. Dual-mode sensing provides a robust approach to achieve enhanced accuracy in detection; however, the utilization of dual-mode detection for C3G remains largely unexplored. The present study introduced a novel, simple, and versatile nanomaterial carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), which harnessed the exceptional spectral properties of CPDs to achieve colorimetric-fluorescence dual-mode sensing of C3G. In the presence of C3G, the fluorescence of CPDs was quenched, resulting in a visible blue color to the naked eye. The utilization of distinct signal generation mechanisms enabled simultaneous acquisition of two independent signals, thereby providing self-verification functionality and enhancing reliability and accuracy. This dual-mode sensor exhibited high specificity, exceled in colorimetric and fluorescence detection, and was well-suited for precise identification of C3G in complex samples. It was poised to emerge as a highly sensitive and dependable tool for food safety analysis. This research project showcased the immense potential of CPDs as an efficient optical sensor while imparting invaluable insights for future C3G detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbonized polymer dots
Photoluminescence mechanism
Cyanidin-3-O-glucoside dual-mode detection
Sensing mechanism

Detection of C3G by the dual-mode assay
Plausible quenching mechanism for the as-prepared CPDs with C3G

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CPDs. (A and B) TEM images, the inset shows the high-resolution TEM image. (C) Hydrodynamic particle size; (D) Zeta potential. (E) XPS survey spectrum; (F) C 1 s, (G) N 1 s, (H) O 1 s high-resolution XPS spectrum with the identification of peaks by curve fitting.

(A) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CPDs. (B) PL emission spectra of the CPDs under excitation at different wavelengths. (C) UV absorption spectra of bare PEI and CPDs. (D) Absolute fluorescence quantum yield of CPDs.
. (A) Normalized PL emission spectra of CPDs in different solvents. (B) Fluorescence spectra of CPDs with different pH.

. (A) Photographic images of CPDs-120, CPDs-160, CPDs-200, CPDs-120 + C3G, CPDs-160 + C3G, CPDs-200 + C3G and C3G under sunlight. (B) Photographic images of CPDs-120, CPDs-160, and CPDs-200 captured on pH indicator paper. (C) Zeta potential of CPDs-120, CPDs-160 and CPDs-200.

. (A) Fluorescence spectra of the proposed sensor toward C3G with various concentrations. (B) Plot of the emission ratio of (FL0-FL)/FL0 vs C3G concentration. Inset: linear relationship between C3G concentration (0.05–25 μg/mL) and (FL0-FL)/FL0. (C) UV–vis absorbance plots in the presence of different levels of C3G. (D) Calibration curve correlating absorbance with C3G concentration. (E) Corresponding photographs of the solutions under UV light of 365 nm (top) and visible light (down).

(A) Colorimetric-fluorescent dual-mode sensor for detecting blank, C3G, and different interferences. (B) The photos of C3G and various interfering substances were taken under UV light (top) and visible light (down) after being introduced into the CPDs solution. (C) The UV–visible absorption spectrum of C3G and various interfering substances was measured after their addition to the CPDs solution. (D) The fluorescence emission spectra of C3G and various interfering substances were measured following the addition to the CPDs solution.
Illustration of CPDs development and its application for colorimetric-fluorescence dual-mode C3G analysis.

head.ce:title: A dual-responsive sensor derived from carbonized polymer dots for the determination of cyanidin-3-O-glucoside

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Medical Technology, Chengdu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Chengdu 611137, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Medical Technology, Chengdu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Accurate detection of cyanidin-3-O-glucoside (C3G) is crucial for ensuring food safety and optimizing quality control measures. Dual-mode sensing provides a robust approach to achieve enhanced accuracy in detection; however, the utilization of dual-mode detection for C3G remains largely unexplored. The present study introduced a novel, simple, and versatile nanomaterial carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), which harnessed the exceptional spectral properties of CPDs to achieve colorimetric-fluorescence dual-mode sensing of C3G. In the presence of C3G, the fluorescence of CPDs was quenched, resulting in a visible blue color to the naked eye. The utilization of distinct signal generation mechanisms enabled simultaneous acquisition of two independent signals, thereby providing self-verification functionality and enhancing reliability and accuracy. This dual-mode sensor exhibited high specificity, exceled in colorimetric and fluorescence detection, and was well-suited for precise identification of C3G in complex samples. It was poised to emerge as a highly sensitive and dependable tool for food safety analysis. This research project showcased the immense potential of CPDs as an efficient optical sensor while imparting invaluable insights for future C3G detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Anthocyanins, as flavonoid phenolic compounds, constitute a distinguished group of natural pigments that are extensively distributed in diverse crops, legumes, vegetables, fruits, and other edible plants commonly consumed by humans on a daily basis [1,2]. Among the diverse array of anthocyanins, cyanidin-3-O-glucoside (C3G) stands out as one of the most prevalent members [3]. It is abundantly present in numerous plant species, particularly berries and fruits, and finds extensive applications in both food and health-related products due to its multifaceted biological activities encompassing anti-cancer properties, anti-inflammatory effects, as well as antioxidant potential [4,5]. Moreover, C3G exhibits hepatoprotective shipattributes while also conferring protective effects on the gastric system, pulmonary organs, intestinal tract, and nervous system [6–8]. The establishment of a rapid, dependable, and highly responsive technique for the detection of C3G in food and health products is imperative. In recent years, a plethora of techniques have been successfully employed in the detection of C3G, including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [9], liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (LC–MS/MS) [10], spectrophotometry [11], and electrochemical [12]. However, these technologies have certain limitations such as expensive equipment, complicated operation and processing procedures, lengthy detection time, skilled technicians and noticeable interference factors [13]. The constraints impede the attainment of swift and efficient analysis in practical applications.

To cater to this demand, chemical/biosensors such as colorimetric sensing have been extensively explored in recent years owing to their convenient result visualization and signal reading capabilities [14]. Despite achieving sensitive determination of flavonoid phenolic compounds, the development of these sensors has been hindered by the limited accuracy of single-mode response readings. The changes triggered by the target analytes are generally associated with a single response, and can be easily influenced by various factors such as diverse personnel operations, unstable experimental environments, substrate morphology, and discrepancies in loads across different batches [15]. Consequently, these factors have the potential to impact the precision and sensitivity of the sensor. To surmount this predicament, a simple and viable solution—a dual-mode sensor platform is proposed. The concept of dual-mode sensing involves the integration of two detection modes within a single system through specific methods or technologies, resulting in the acquisition of diverse and complementary data from samples. This integration ultimately enhances the precision and quality of test results. Notably, dual-mode analysis quantifies target analytes through the synchronous activation of two distinct types of physico-chemical signals triggered by targets using different detectors, rather than employing diverse methodologies to measure the same sample [16]. In this case, each signal is independent and undisturbed, thereby mitigating fluctuations in the analysis data within the complex sample matrix. This facilitates mutual verification and reduces susceptibility to various coexistence interferences, operator variations, or non-standard instruments and procedures. Consequently, it enhances the accuracy, reliability, and diversity of detection outcomes [17]. Moreover, if there exists a discrepancy in sensitivity between the two employed detection methods, it enables widening of the detection range for target analytes to accomplish preliminary screening and sensitive identification of samples. Additionally, the implementation of dual-mode detection serves to address the limitations inherent in single-mode analysis, including diminished capacity for information collection, elevated rates of laboratory testing consumption, and compromised levels of accuracy and reliability [16,18]. For example, enhanced by the fluorescence characteristics and light-activated oxidase activity of thioflavin T, Guo et al. [19] ingeniously devised a dual-mode (fluorescence and colorimetric) aptamer method for exquisitely sensitive and reliably accurate detection of estradiol. The application of this dual-mode method had yielded successful results in detecting estradiol in river water and meat, showcasing its promising potential in the realms of environmental and food safety. The integration of a wide range of nanoparticles, including metal-organic frame nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), noble metal nanomaterials and quantum dots (QDs), with dual-signal sensors has been seamlessly achieved to enhance the analytical performance [20].

Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) exhibit tremendous potential in fluorescence sensing, disease diagnosis and treatment, nanomedicines and in vivo imaging owing to their high photoluminescence quantum yield, remarkable water solubility, unparalleled stability and biocompatibility, as well as environmental friendliness [21]. CPDs has been refined to a distinct subclass of carbon dots which characterized by a carbonized core enveloped within an elegant polymer structure. The carbon cores are typically composed of highly dehydrated crosslinking polymer frameworks or slight graphitization, combined with abundant functional groups and polymer chains to formulate the outer shells through processes involving dehydration, polymerization, and carbonization. It is truly encouraging that the distinctive carbon/polymer core-shell structure and exceptional photoluminescence mechanism ensure a remarkably controllable structure-performance relationship of CPDs, achieved through precise adjustments to both the carbon core and the polymer chain enriched with functional groups [22,23]. Xu et al. employed a simple and cost-effective hydrothermal technique to synthesize CPDs, upon which they successfully developed an intelligent sensing system capable of capturing the fluorescent color variations on the paper strip via mobile phone-based color recognition software, thereby enabling real-time determination of Cu2+ and S2- concentrations [24]. However, to the best of our knowledge, there is currently a dearth of research on the detection of C3G using CPDs-based dual-mode sensors. Furthermore, the intricate precursor and diverse synthesis methodologies have augmented the intricacy in unraveling the precise chemical composition and luminescent mechanism of CPDs.

In this study, we have introduced an innovative CPDs-based C3G colorimetric and fluorescence dual-mode sensor for the first time, showcasing its fast response, heightened sensitivity and selectivity in detecting C3G. The synthesis of the CPDs from polyethylenimine (PEI) as the starting material, involving crosslink, oxidation, polymerization, carbonization and other intricate processes, was elegantly depicted in Scheme 1. The structural and morphological characteristics of these CPDs were meticulously elucidated through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), transmission electron microscope (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and other advanced analytical techniques. Furthermore, an exhaustive investigation into their optical properties, photoluminescence mechanism and stability was conducted. By utilizing the structural characteristics and exceptional optical properties of CPDs, a dual-mode colorimetric-fluorescence detection system for C3G was ingeniously devised. The performance of this sensor was thoroughly investigated in terms of its linear range, selectivity, and real sample detection capability, while also delving into the underlying sensing mechanism.
The reagents and instruments are available in the Supplementary materials S1.1.

Blue fluorescent CPDs with excellent sensing performance were synthesized via a classical one-pot hydrothermal reaction. The precursor solution was first prepared by adding 0.2 g BPEI into 20 mL ultrapure water with thorough stirring until completely dissolved. Then, the precursor solution was transferred to an autoclave and heated to 160 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was purified by dialysis against ultrapure water through a 3500 Da dialysis membrane to obtain CPDs. The CPDs solution were stored at -20 °C in the dark until used in subsequent experiments, and its concentration was acquired by a freeze-drying method. CPDs-120 and CPDs-200 were also synthesized according to the above procedure, except that the reaction temperature was different (at 120 °C and 200 °C, respectively). Unless otherwise specified, “CPDs” in this following refer to carbonized polymer dots synthesized by reaction at 160 °C.

Different concentrations of C3G solution (10 μL) and 1.99 mL CPDs were added into a quartz cell with a path length of 1 cm. After equilibrium, the fluorescence spectra of blank (CPDs) and mixed solution (CPDs + C3G) were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm. The detection method of UV–VIS spectroscopy was basically the same as that of fluorescence. The only difference was that the concentration of CPDs was diluted 10 times before use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of C3G in black rice
ce:section.ce:para: Black rice was pulverized using a high-speed grinder and subsequently sieved through an 80 mesh sieve. 0.5 g black rice powder was dissolved in 5 mL of methanol and subjected to ultrasonication for 30 minutes under lightless conditions, followed by centrifugation at a speed of 3000 rpm/min for 5 minutes. The solid obtained after centrifugation was subjected to repeated extraction using the aforementioned method. The resulting supernatant was combined with the initial extract and filtered through a 0.22 µm filter membrane to obtain the required black rice extract, which should be stored at -20 ℃ in a refrigerator for preservation.

The CPDs were synthesized via hydrothermal method at 160 ℃ using PEI as the raw material, and their morphology and structure were initially analyzed by TEM. The CPDs particles, depicted in Figs. 1A and 1B, exhibited an average diameter of approximately 67 nm and displayed a uniform size distribution. These particles had a nearly spherical morphology with a dense polymer characteristic on their surface. High-resolution lattice fringes ranging from 0.19 to 0.22 nm were observed in the high-resolution TEM image of CPD (Fig. 1B), corresponding to the transition of polymer structure towards a carbon core structure [25]. However, the lattice fringe spacing of CPDs was different from that of graphite ((100) plane with a lattice fringe spacing of 0.21 nm), which could be attributed to the insertion of polymer chains within graphite structures, resulting in distinct lattice spaces [26]. Therefore, the observed lattice structure was derived not only from graphite lattice, but also from the well-organized and compact polymer framework [27].

Figs. 1C and 1D depicted the hydrodynamic size and zeta potential of CPDs at neutral pH. The hydrodynamic size (Fig. 1C), as determined by dynamic light scattering was found to be significantly larger (341.3 nm) compared observed size in TEM images due to the presence of polymer chains on the surface of CPDs. The zeta potential measurement results revealed a positive zeta potential of + 14.6 mV for CPDs in the meantime (Fig. 1D).

In order to investigate the elemental composition and chemical bonding of CPDs, XPS spectroscopy analysis was conducted on CPDs, as illustrated in Fig. 1E. CPDs were composed of three elements: carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O). The atomic ratio of C was significantly high at 70.14% (20.67% for N and 9.19% for O), indicating the occurrence of carbonization during the formation process of CPDs. Moreover, the presence of oxygen in CPDs suggested that environmental oxygen might be involved in the entire carbonization process. The high-resolution spectrum analysis revealed that the C1s spectrum could be deconvoluted into three distinct peaks at 284.8, 284.3, and 287.1 eV (Fig. 1F), corresponding to CC, C−C, and CO respectively. The high-resolution N1s spectrum revealed the presence of C-N-C (399.2 eV) and CN-C (398.3 eV) peaks, as depicted in Fig. 1G. The O1s peaks observed in Fig. 1H were located at 530.4 and 531.6 eV, respectively, indicating the predominant presence of oxygen in the form of CO and N-C-O.

The FTIR characterization results (Fig. S1) further validated the involvement of oxygen in the carbonization process of CPDs, suggesting that the oxidation of amino groups in PEI monomers may contribute to the formation of CPDs. It was anticipated that, under the influence of oxygen during heating, oxidative reactions occur at the secondary amine sites within PEI monomers, leading to the generation of amide groups in CPDs [23].

The PL spectra of CPDs ( Fig. 2A) revealed that the maximum emission and excitation of CPDs occurred at 340 nm and 465 nm respectively, and exhibited a typical excitation-dependent emission (Fig. 2B). These emissions came from different FL centers and complex energy levels, indicating that the existence of different photoluminescence states in CPDs lead to excitation dependent emission. At the same time, multiple energy level transitions would occur in the multi-dispersed fluorescence center under an excitation wavelength, resulting in a large FWHM of emission [28]. The absorption spectrum of CPDs was illustrated in Fig. 2C, revealing a prominent absorption band at 369 nm, which could be attributed to the n-π* transition of the carbonyl moiety [29]. Conversely, PEI demonstrated negligible absorption beyond 260 nm. Upon exposure to 360 nm ultraviolet light, CPDs demonstrated a conspicuous blue fluorescence, contrasting with their pale yellow appearance under ambient lighting conditions (inset of Fig. 2C). Additionally, the absolute quantum yield (QY) of CPDs was determined to be 6.13%, as depicted in Fig. 2D.

Currently, the elucidation of the photoluminescence (PL) mechanism in fluorescent carbonized polymer dots remains a subject of ongoing scholarly discourse. Quantum confinement effect or conjugated π-domains, surface state, molecular state, and crosslink-enhanced emission (CEE) effect are currently widely acknowledged and reasonably plausible mechanisms for photoluminescence [30,31]. The quantum confinement effect or conjugated π-domain is determined by the carbon core, typically originating from the intrinsic emission of carbon core with a perfect graphite structure [32]. In this study, the high-resolution TEM images of CPDs did not exhibit distinct lattice fringes and showed a relatively low number of observed lattice fringes, which deviated from the expected graphite lattice structure. The presence of encapsulated and intercalated polymer chains might impede the observation of the crystalline carbon core.

Surface states are not independent chemical groups, but a hybridization of the carbon skeleton and connected chemical groups (sometimes referred to as subfluorophores). The functional groups on the surface of carbonized polymers have a rich energy level structure, capable of forming a series of emission traps. Through higher degrees of surface oxidation or other effective modification methods, more surface states can be formed on carbonized polymer dots, resulting in the generation of new sub-levels and consequent alterations in emission [32]. As depicted in Fig. 3A, the emission wavelength of the synthesized CPDs exhibited a red shift with increasing solvent polarity. Furthermore, the fluorescence emission of CPDs exhibited significant sensitivity to variations in pH, displaying optimal performance under weakly alkaline conditions (pH = 8; see Fig. 3B). Conversely, an acidic environment rendered CPDs insoluble. The different PL intensities of CPDs at various pH values might be attributed to the surface chemistry of CPDs [33]. Process of oxidation facilitated the generation of oxidation defects on the surface of CPDs, thereby inducing intricate surface states that effectively capture excitons upon excitation. The resulting radiation composite of these excitons captured at the surface gave rise to photoluminescence emission with corresponding energy [34].

Molecular-state luminescence typically exhibits the following characteristics [35–37]: (1) The UV–visible absorption spectra of molecular fluorophores consistently exhibit distinct Gaussian peaks. (2) The emission wavelength remains constant and is independent of variations in excitation. (3) Molecular fluorophores possess relatively low molecular weights and can readily permeate dialysis membranes, thus extensive dialysis can significantly diminish molecular-state fluorescence. The CPDs synthesized in this work, as depicted in Fig. 2B and 2C, exhibited a non-Gaussian peak type and demonstrated pronounced excitation-dependent emission. Furthermore, an investigation was conducted into the impact of dialysis on the fluorescence of CPDs, as illustrated in Figure S2. After dialysis, the CPDs solution exhibited intense fluorescence emission within the dialysis bag (membrane), while no significant fluorescence signal was detected in the dialysate outside the dialysis bag (membrane), indicating an absence of molecular fluorophores.

The crosslinking enhanced emission (CEE) effect was initially proposed to elucidate the intense emission of non-conjugated polymer dots (NCPDs), which exclusively bind to sub-fluorophores [22,38]. The CPDs synthesized in this work contained sub-fluorophores such as CN, CO, and amino-based groups with single bonds. These sub-fluorophores could be effectively immobilized, leading to significant suppression of chromophore vibration and rotation, inhibition of non-radiative transitions, and enhancement of luminescent emission [39]. Moreover, the tight fixation prevented intermolecular stacking between chromophores in CPDs through π-π interactions, thereby avoiding luminescence quenching. Additionally, crosslinking reduced the distance between sub-fluorophores clusters, resulting in a substantial overlap of electron clouds that might generate cluster-like luminescence [40,41].
In summary, the photoluminescence (PL) mechanism of prepared CPDs was expected to be primarily governed by surface states and CEE effects.

Furthermore, we have observed a fascinating phenomenon. The color of C3G alone was observed to be pink, as depicted in Figure S3; however, upon the addition of CPDs, the solution rapidly transitioned to a blue-purple hue. Moreover, upon exposure to ultraviolet light irradiation, the fluorescence intensity of CPDs decreased with increasing amounts of C3G present. Leveraging this characteristic enabled the realization of a dual-mode detection system for C3G, combining colorimetric and fluorescence techniques. Nevertheless, inconsistent variations in color were observed upon reaction of C3G with CPD solutions synthesized at different temperatures (120 °C, 160 °C and 200 °C), as depicted in Fig. 4A. Specifically, as the synthesis temperature of CPDs increased, there was a gradual transition towards a pinkish-brown shade noted. To further investigate this phenomenon, pH testing papers were utilized to assess the acidity levels of CPDs-120, CPDs-160 and CPDs-200 respectively (Fig. 4B); revealing an increase in acidity corresponding to higher synthesis temperatures. Anthocyanins exhibit pH-dependent structural transformations, leading to alterations in their coloration behavior [42]. Under acidic conditions, C3G appeared orange or red and existed in the form of flavylium cations. As the pH increased, a competition aroused between kinetic and thermodynamic reactions involving hydration of the flavylium cation and proton transfer reactions associated with the acidic hydroxyl groups of the aglycone. The former reaction yielded a colorless carbinol pseudo-base, which could undergo ring opening to generate a chalcone pseudo-base, whereas the latter reactions led to the formation of quinonoidal bases. The quinonoidal bases further underwent deprotonation within a pH range from neutral to weakly alkaline, resulting in the formation of resonance-stabilized purple quinone anions [43,44]. The inconsistent pH values observed for CPDs-120, CPDs-160, and CPDs-200 might be attributed to the oxidative degradation of amino groups. It was postulated that the amino group of PEI underwent oxidative reactions in the presence of oxygen during the heating process, resulting in varying degrees of amino group oxidation at different temperatures and subsequently leading to distinct properties exhibited by CPDs. Furthermore, the zeta potentials of CPDs-120, CPDs-160, and CPDs-200 were evaluated. As illustrated in Fig. 4C, a gradual decrease in zeta potential was observed with increasing temperature, providing evidence for the oxidation of amino groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of C3G by the dual-mode assay
ce:section.ce:para:
The CPDs exhibited excellent stability and remarkable resistance to oxidation, as demonstrated in the Supplementary materials S2.1 and S2.2. The dual-mode sensing performance of CPDs was evaluated by employing colorimetric and fluorescence methods for quantitative analysis of C3G. The fluorescence detection was based on the phenomenon of inner filter effect, wherein the decrease in fluorescence intensity demonstrated a direct proportionality to the concentration of C3G. As illustrated in Fig. 5A, the fluorescence of CPDs at 465 nm gradually diminished with increasing C3G concentration within the range of 0–95 µg/mL. The relationship between (FL
0
-FL)/FL
0 and the concentration of C3G ranging from 0.05 to 25 µg/mL, as shown in Fig. 5B, exhibited a strong linear correlation. The coefficient of determination (R
2 ) was calculated to be 0.9914, while the limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 10 ng/mL with a signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) of 3. Colorimetric detection performance of the sensor was investigated in Fig. 5C and D. The absorbance value exhibited a gradual increase with increasing C3G concentration. Within the range of 0.5–40 µg/mL, a linear regression equation between the absorbance value and C3G concentration was established as A = 0.0306 × C
C3G + 0.0239, with an R
2 value of 0.991, while achieving a detection limit of 0.2 µg/mL. The color changes of CPDs under ultraviolet light and natural light were vividly illustrated in Fig. 5E after the addition of varying concentrations of C3G. The alteration in color signal could be easily observed, enabling effortless visual detection of C3G. To further explore the testing performance of sensors on C3G, we conducted a comparison of various testing methods in Table S1. The results demonstrate that this dual-mode sensor effectively combines fluorescence-sensitive detection and visual prediction, providing a wide linear range and low detection limit, thereby enhancing its suitability for tracking and detecting C3G. Additionally, the mutual validation provided by dual-mode detection significantly improves the accuracy of C3G detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plausible quenching mechanism for the as-prepared CPDs with C3G
ce:section.ce:para: The investigation was conducted to explore the potential mechanisms by which C3G can effectively quench the fluorescence of CPDs. Among the commonly observed quenching mechanisms are Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), dynamic quenching, static quenching, and inner filter effect (IFE). Firstly, the absorption spectrum of C3G was investigated, along with the examination of excitation and emission spectrum of CPDs. The ultraviolet absorption spectrum of C3G, as depicted in Figure S6A, exhibited a partial overlap with the emission spectrum of CPDs. However, this observation did not provide conclusive evidence regarding whether the quenching effect of C3G on CPDs fluorescence was due to internal filtration, fluorescence energy transfer, static quenching or dynamic quenching. The fluorescence lifetime of CPDs was 4.38 ns, and upon the addition of C3G to the CPDs solution, it was 4.29 ns (Fig. S6B). Remarkably, the presence or absence of C3G scarcely altered the fluorescence lifetime of CPDs, thereby negating any possibility of FRET and dynamic quenching effects. In FRET and dynamic quenching phenomena, it is common for the fluorescence lifetime of fluorophores to undergo changes upon encountering a quencher [45]. Subsequently, we delved into the ultraviolet absorption spectra of CPDs, C3G, and CPDs+C3G (Fig. S6C). The study revealed that upon adding C3G to the CPDs solution, significant changes occurred in the absorption spectra of CPDs+C3G with new peaks emerging or characteristic peaks shifting towards longer wavelengths. The observed phenomenon indicated the formation of a ground state complex, thereby excluding the possibility of IFE. During the process of IFE, it is crucial that the UV absorption peak of fluorescent substances remains unaltered even in the presence of a quencher [45]. Therefore, it was highly plausible that the fluorescence quenching of CPDs by C3G could be attributed to static quenching.

To evaluate the selectivity of the developed sensor, we conducted interference experiments utilizing a variety of substances commonly encountered in similar studies, which have the potential to impede accurate detection of the target analyte C3G or are frequently present in samples associated with C3G analysis. The obtained results were showcased in Fig. 6. As depicted in Fig. 6A and B, the experimental results demonstrated that when only interference was present, they aligned with those of the blank experiment. Notably, the dual-mode sensor exhibited significantly higher responsiveness to C3G compared to other interferences, indicating its excellent selectivity for C3G. Furthermore, analysis of absorption spectra and emission spectra (Fig. 6C and D) of CPDs solution before and after introducing various interferences further confirmed negligible impact on the CPDs system by these interferences, thus affirming the superior selectivity of the developed dual-mode sensor. The quantitative analysis of the dual-mode sensor in the actual sample of black rice also demonstrated the applicability of the developed dual-mode sensor, which seamlessly integrated fluorescence and colorimetric detection for precise sample analysis. The results and discussions are elaborated in the Supplementary materials S2.3.

In summary, the CPDs with a unique structure, abundant functional groups, and exceptional stability had been successfully synthesized through a straightforward and cost-effective hydrothermal method. Furthermore, the luminescence mechanism of CPDs was meticulously investigated, aiming to unravel the intricate puzzle of their precise chemical composition and luminescence mechanism, given the complexity of their precursors and diverse synthesis methods. Based on this premise, a novel colorimetric-fluorescence dual-mode sensor was successfully developed for quantitative analysis of C3G. This study represents the first report on utilizing CPDs to construct a C3G sensor and its successful application in detecting C3G in black rice. The utilization of this sophisticated multi-channel sensor effectively mitigates the impact of intricate food matrices, thereby minimizing both false negatives and false positives, while simultaneously yielding more dependable and precise test outcomes. Furthermore, the detection signal acquired by this dual-mode sensor can be subjected to rigorous statistical analysis to authenticate the reliability of the results obtained. Undoubtedly, this self-verifying dual-mode sensor will play a pivotal role in proactively preventing and substantially reducing instances of food safety breaches. The advantages of CPDs and dual-mode detection, in addition to their inherent benefits, have also established a robust foundation for real-time detection applications on portable and compact devices.

Zhentao Tang: Supervision, Resources. Hulan Chen: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Yang Li: Project administration, Methodology. Yueping You: Validation. Yingshuang Wang: Formal analysis. Tingzhi Yang: Investigation. Kaixin Chen: Validation, Software, Investigation. Yu Fan: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 82373986, 82204616), Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province (Grant No. 2022NSFSC1475, 2022NSFSC1584), and Xinglin Scholar Research Promotion Project of Chengdu University of TCM (Grant No. MPRC2022027).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.136029.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yu Fan is a lecturer at College of Medical Technology, Chengdu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine. Her research interests encompass electrochemical biosensors, ECL sensors, and optical sensors. She has accumulated extensive expertise in the field of biosensing technology and food safety detection.
Kaixin Chen is currently studying at College of Medical Technology, Chengdu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine. Her research area is the application of carbon nanomaterials in bacteriostasis.
Tingzhi Yang is currently studying at College of Medical Technology, Chengdu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine. Her research focuses on the construction of dual-mode sensors.
Yingshuang Wang is professor at College of Medical Technology, Chengdu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine. Her research focuses on optical biosensors.

Yueping You is currently studying for her MS degree at School of Pharmacy, Chengdu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine. Her research interest is the application of optical sensors in the analysis of traditional Chinese medicine.
Yang Li is professor at School of Public Health, Chengdu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine. Her research focuses on the application of sensors in food safety analysis.

Hulan Chen is professor at College of Medical Technology, Chengdu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine. Her research interest is the application of optical sensors in the analysis of traditional Chinese medicine.
Zhentao Tang is a lecturer at College of Medical Technology, Chengdu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine. Her research interests include studying nanomaterials and biosensors.
